speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken off the first transport. I was absolutely traumatized because the other children in my group-- the other boys-- all my age group, that-- they all went on the first transport. And basically what had happened by then is we had formed like, families. And I just thought my second family. I was absolutely-- it was horrendous to me. You have no idea how much I cried. In any case, they left and I was left with some other children. And then we were taken out of Chateau Montintin and transferred to another Chateau, which they felt was safer, also near-- near-- near Limoges, called Chateau [FRENCH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was only there for a very short time, and then we were transferred back to Chateau Montintin. I do not know the reasons for that. But in approximately August, they suddenly called me in and said, OK, you're ready. You're-- you're going on the next transport. I was lucky enough to be one of the 100 children to be chosen for the second transport. We all went down to Marseilles. And the second transport was broken up into, actually, two separate transports. 45 kids came on the second transport, or part A of the second transport. And the other 55 on part B of the second transport. I was on part A. I was on the first part of the-- of the second transport. I arrived to the United States in June of 1941. I'm sorry. September 1st, 1941. I was placed in a small orphan-- an orphan-- what had been an orphanage in New York City, on Amsterdam Avenue and 138th Street, which eventually became part of City College of New York. And later on, I attended classes there, actually, in that same building. After about a week in the orphanage, they took the 45 of us and we were separated. Everybody was find someplace else. We all went into either-- they sent us all over the United States, either into foster homes or into-- or into orphanages, but we were all separated. I was-- I was sent to a foster home in the Bronx with a couple who had three children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was, again, that first night was probably one of the worst nights of my life. I had lost a second family my-- when the-- when the first transport left France. And now I was separated from the rest of them. Here I was in a home, a home I didn't know anybody. I didn't speak any English. I had-- totally alone. I mean, I felt so totally alone, it was-- it was almost unbearable. But slowly but surely, you-- you come around. And, you know, life continues. I was enrolled in school. I started learning English. The foster home, unfortunately, did not work out very well. And it is not necessarily a fault of the foster parents. But I know a lot of the kids now who went into foster homes and most of them did not work out. Basically, most of the foster homes were people-- foster parents were people who were on the low economic income group. And when they took a foster child, they were being paid by the state. And they needed an income to help out. And it basically was a place to stay. I got room and board.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was obvious to me that the treatment that I received-- it wasn't bad. I don't want anyone to think that the treatment was bad. But I was not a child like the other children. I mean, things I remember is that I couldn't go to the refrigerator, for instance, without asking, which they constantly did. OK? So, I mean, there were things of that nature. But you survive these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I started going to-- to school. I was, at the time, 12 and 1/2. No, it was a month before my 13th birthday. And I started learning English at-- I was put in the fifth grade but because there was a teacher there who spoke Yiddish. So we were able to communicate. Anyway, I started picking up school. Well, a month after I got here, it was my 13th birthday. It was my bar mitzvah, so I asked my foster parents to-- I wanted to have a bar mitzvah. Which they-- which the temple that they belonged to made for me. It was all paid by charitable contributions, I'm sure. I remember there were two other boys having their bar mitzvah on the same day as I did. And of course they had parents, they had parties, they had gifts. And I felt very much alone. I got a suit from the Jewish Childcare Association whose auspices I was under for my bar mitzvah. And it was Knickerbocker's which was, at that time, out-moded and I felt very self-conscious about the whole thing. But, you know, you live. You live through these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: After about a year or so, this aunt and uncle-- the uncle who had been in Buchenwald had come to the United States. Of course, they were very poor at the time also. They couldn't really afford to take me. I convinced the Jewish Childcare Association that if they can pay for my-- if they can pay my foster parents, why can't they pay my aunt and uncle and I can stay with them? Which, eventually, that's exactly what they did and I started staying with my aunt and uncle. How did your aunt and uncle find you? Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just knew it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an address because-- I don't remember how I had the address, but I had an address for my aunt and uncle. And somehow the Jewish Organization-- the OSE had an-- had the address also, because among the papers, I-- I saw that-- that I have an uncle and aunt living in Kingston, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, you had received this last letter from your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point-- no. At this point, yeah. I received the last letter from my dad approximately two months after my bar mitzvah. I was-- it was in October, it was about December or so when I received the letter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In any case, I started living with my aunt and uncle. I started growing up as a teenager. I was accepted to Stuyvesant High School in New York, so I went to, you know, a special technical high school. After I finished high school, I had decided that I really don't have anybody here. My aunt and uncle were fine, but they had a daughter of their own. I was growing up. It was not my parents. I decided, well, maybe I'll go to Israel. OK. Well. Then I decided, well, I think I should go to college first. So I enrolled at City College of New York and that's where I met my wife, Trudy. We were both engineering class students, and we were sitting next to each other in a mathematics class in differential equations. And she, getting her normal A's, and me, getting my normal C's, I decided maybe I should study with her. So that's how we met, and we both graduated in 1952. By the way, while I was going to school, I was always working. I had all kinds of jobs. In the summer, I worked as a waiter up in the Catskills. In the wintertime, I had various jobs. Amongst them, as strange as it may seem, was a dancing instructor at Arthur Murray studios. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had part time jobs, so I supported myself through school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When-- when we graduated school, we came out to California because I really didn't have much in New York. My uncle had died. My aunt and I really weren't that close, so I really had very little in New York. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in my last year of school, yes, in 1951, and we graduated in 1952. Came out to California and started raising a family. And today-- then I started working for-- for Lockheed at the time, but I eventually went to Rocketdyne and worked in the aerospace industry developing rocket engines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We now have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your children's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son, his name is Aaron. He's a physician, and his wife is Leslie, and they've got two children, Rachel and Shira. My middle boy, his name is David. His wife's name is Naina, and they have two children. One is Alex and one is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Samantha. And my youngest one-- by the way, he's an insurance agent. And my youngest one, Daniel, he's single and he's an attorney. And they're all living in the greater Los Angeles area. So we have a very good relationship between all our children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your experiences a lot to your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as they were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There's a book-- there were-- have been several books written about my experiences. One of them is which I have a copy of in case anybody would like to see it. It's no longer in publication. It tells about the experience in France with the OSE. There are other books that have been written. One I'm reading right now, it's called-- it's a-- it's a book in French, actually. It's about the OSE before, during, and after the war. The OSE homes, what they did, and it mentions in there-- it specifically states about the children's transport, how they came to France, what the OSE did. They fact that they-- the fact that they-- this particular transport that I was on is mentioned. About the 45 children, about the Quakers. I mean, it's-- it's all documented there. So there is a lot of documentation around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you found out the fate of your family for sure by writing, later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after-- yeah. After the war, after I came to the United States, I still would write to Switzerland through our contact. But no further letters came from my parents after that letter. After the war, I tried to find out what happened with them through the-- through the Red Cross and through anybody else I could find. And there was no trace anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know that-- you know-- they knew, for instance, where-- the addresses of my aunt and uncle, so that they could have gotten in contact with somebody had they been alive. So I'm very sure that they were-- they were all killed. The relatives who did survive-- my father's brother, and his wife, and my cousin, Otto, but they fled to Chile.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found them again about 1961, 1962. We were looking for each other. It was a very accidental finding. But we had a-- a relative on my mother's side-- a distant relative-- who had business in Chile. And I met them one time and told them about an uncle I had there. And the person that this particular man went to see in Chile turns out to be a friend of my uncle's, and that's how we found each other. Several other cousin survived. Like I say, the aunt and uncle-- this is my mother's sisters, the one who-- my uncle who was in Buchenwald-- came to the United States. And their daughter also eventually came to United States from-- from England. I have another cousin who fled to Italy. He was already 15 years older than I was. So he was already grown man and became a physician over there. He survived the war, and after the war, came to the United States with his Italian wife. And his sister was sent on the children's transport to-- to England, and she survived the war. And she's living in New York now. His sister, which is, of course, my cousin, Deta-- I mean, she-- she-- for a long time, she-- she still has guilt feelings today. Because when her parents sent her on the children's transport, she felt like her parents were trying to get rid of her. And she just wouldn't communicate with her parents at all until she found out exactly what-- she grew up and found out that they really saved her life. And she had guilt-- and her parents were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all my father's brothers-- the other four brothers in Poland, the whole family, everybody was killed. Children, families, so-- and that pretty much is my story. I have been very-- I'm now retired. I-- I have been very, very happy here in the United States. I think about my experiences a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a group of friends-- those children from these children's homes. We have been finding each other for the past-- since we came to the United States. There's a group of about 25 of us or 30-- excuse me-- or 30 of us living in the Los Angeles area, all the way up to San Diego, San Francisco. Another group in New York. And in 1989, I organized a reunion, and 70 of the original kids showed up. They came from 15 states, two from Mexico, one from Israel, and we heard from others who did not attend from Australia, from England, from Switzerland, from you name it. Additionally, every year, we try to have one party for-- for the group. And in fact, next August, this coming Aug-- this coming August. I think it's August 26th. We-- we've already sent the letters out that we're having a party. We'll have about 40 people here. The-- most of my friends are from that group. Either-- either the man or the woman was one of the children. And the spouses are-- you know, most of them are American spouses. But we all have become-- it's become a family. It's really not-- it's really not just friends. It's-- it's much more than that. We see each other constantly, either, you know-- either on a social basis. Maybe two of us will go the theater, or a whole group get together for one evening, or we play cards, or whatever it is. But we have been-- we, like I said, we have been finding each other for a long, long time. In fact, the last-- the last one was just found living approximately within walking distance of here. We found-- my wife found them by going to-- she goes and has-- has muffins every morning, I guess, and started talking to somebody over there. Turns out that he was in one of the OSE homes. And here's the first time that he's going to come to one of our get togethers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time you meet one of these people, it's really a shock to them. By the way, I found-- the last two or three years, I found two people who came out of the same children's transports from Vienna to-- out to France. They went-- those two, instead of going to OSE, they went first to Chateau [FRENCH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They came over on the first transport. One of them became Vice President of TRW of engineering. The other one eventually wound up in Australia and became the Senior Trade Representative for the Australian government and traveled all over the world. They're living in Los Angeles And they want to be at out party. What is surprising about that whole group of children is the success rate. They are-- you name the profession, or-- and it's just amazing. We have doctors. We have lawyers. We have engineers. We have accountants. There are several business people. There are at least three or four millionaires from these children. So it's-- it's a-- it's a very success-oriented group. It's-- it really amazes me that kids who started up with absolutely nothing made something of their lives. And most of these people are very upbeat. There are very few who are-- who are really-- I mean, we all remember our past, some more than others. I don't think a day goes by when I don't think about something about, you know, originally either my home or something in France. But generally speaking, this whole group is very upbeat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your remarkable story. We're going to look at some pictures you have now, Arthur.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is a photograph of my grandfather, with me sitting on his lap, taken in 1930. Approximately 1930. It is taken in Austria, in a town called Heidenreichstein, which is in the northern part of Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Chaim Goldfeld-- Goldfeld. Hask-- Haskal Goldfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my grandmother, Hinda Goldfeld, taken approximately 1930 in her apartment in Vienna in-- which was in the second district in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my family-- of my immediate family and my mother's sister's family, taken approximately 1935. From left, my aunt Anna. Her husband, Sigmund, who is the one who went to Buchenwald and-- and did get out and eventually came to the United States. Their daughter, Jeanette, who is still alive and lives in New York. My mother, Frida, my brother, Fritz, my father, Herman, and myself. Taken in Heidenreichstein, which is in northern Austria. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a release certificate from the concentration camp, Buchenwald. If you look in the upper left hand corner, it says, Konzentrationslager, Buchenwald, which is the concentration camp of Buchenwald. In heavy printing, Entlassungsschein means release certificate. It is for my uncle. It says, Jude, would means Jew, [GERMAN] And he was-- and it's the date-- I can't read here, but I think it's 19-- yes, it must be 1939. The original of this document, is, along with all my other documents-- letters from my parents-- are with the Simon Wiesenthal Center. In the center-- lower center-- you will see the German stamp with the [GERMAN], with the-- with the crooked cross, with their-- with the-- with their insignia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This document is the application which allowed me to go from Austria into France on the children's transport. It is from the Comite Israelite Pour Les Enfants, Venant D'Allemagne Et De L'Europe Centrale, which means from-- from Germany and from-- it's-- it's the committee for children who are coming from Germany and from central France. The next line, it lists my father's name, Kernberg, Samuel Hirsch. And underneath it where it says 'l'enfant,' is my name. Kernberg, Oswald, and the date I was born. And near the bottom, in typing, it says in French, the father has sold everything in order for the family to survive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken in France in 1940 in one of the children's homes. From left to right is Jacob Hirsch, his brother, Anzam Hirsch, and myself. Jacob Hirsch is today an accountant living in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mississippi, I believe. His brother, Anzam Hirsch, is a rabbi who is in Israel. And myself. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a photograph of the same three people you saw in the last photograph taken in 1989 where we had organized a reunion where we found 70 of the original group. On the left is Jack Hirsch. He's the one who became an accountant-- oh, it's Atlanta, Georgia where he is. His brother, Anzam, who is now today Asher Hirsch, who is in Israel, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken here in Los Angeles, at one of the temples on Pico, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken of me in 1941, just before I left France. Interesting thing about the photograph, which I remember very well, is this sweater that I'm wearing which I knitted myself. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This photograph is of the house that we lived in when I was in Chateau Montintin. This is the ancillary house, the Chateau Montintin. They called it the che-- la chevrette, which means the little goat. There were 40 children living in this house. There were four rooms for boys, approximately six boys to a room, and three rooms for the girls, approximately six girls to each room. Photograph was taken after the war, when we went back to visit the place, and it's taken approximately 1983 or so. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken from a newspaper article of when the children-- when we arrived in September-- of September 19-- September 1st, 1941. I am on this picture. I'm on the right-hand side, and the third one down from the top. I'm sort of a blonde looking kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City, yes. When we arrived in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Amongst my souvenirs is this little piece of this card, which is a-- with my number on it. Number 23. We each, who-- who was sent on the children's transport to the United States, we were each assigned a number in Marseilles and given one of these cards from the American-- American Friends, which is a Quaker organization who helped us get to the United States. My number was 23, And we had to wear this around our neck from the time that we left Marseilles until we arrived in Fra-- in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a photograph of my family taken in 1989, during a reunion which we organized for all the children who survived that we found here in the United States. From left to right, my oldest son, Aaron, his wife, Leslie, my mi-- my youngest son, Daniel, my wife, Trudy, myself, my middle son, David, and his wife, Nina. The placard in front is different memorabilia that different people from-- brought to the reunion with their own pictures from France or from-- from-- from-- well, their remembrances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, yes. Taken in Los Angeles on Pico Boulevard. I forgot the name of the temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my whole family as it exists today, taken approximately a month ago. From left to right, my daughter-in-law, Leslie, my son, Daniel, Shira, which is one of my granddaughters, my son, David, holding another granddaughter Rachel, my other daughter-in-law, Nina, with Samantha, and my oldest son, Aaron. And in the front is my grandson, Alex.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in my son Aaron's house in Seal Beach, approximately a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about June of '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: About June of '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is a picture of my wife and myself taken in New York in May of '95. We were going to attend a-- the wedding of my cousin's youngest daughter. My cousin, the one who escaped to Chile and then wound up in-- in the United States.
